text: This video presents a laparoscopic detorsion of an ilioanal J pouch. We have no disclosures to make. Restorative proctocolectomy with ilial pouch anal anastomosis or IPAA, which was first introduced in nineteen seventy eight, has been the surgical treatment of choice for patients with ulcerative colitis who are medically refractory or in those patients with dysplasia. Because of the widespread adoption of laparoscopic surgery, the outcomes for this procedure have been proven to be better than those with the open technique. Even though the technique has improved, complications are still present and can include small bowel obstruction, pouch leakage, anastomotic stricture, fistula formation, and pouchitis, to name a few.

During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes clear free fluid in the abdomen and dilated small bowel extending to the pelvis. The ileoanal J pouch is visualized, revealing a 360-degree twist involving the pouch and its mesentery. The uterus is lifted to further assess the situation, and the bowel appears uncompromised.

An additional trocar is placed for the assistant surgeon to aid in grasping. The J-pouch is gently manipulated and detorsed, revealing correct positioning without twisting. The mesentery shows no intrinsic twisting. Adhesions between the distal J-pouch and pelvis are sharply lysed. The presacral space appears large, potentially contributing to the pouch twisting. The small bowel is examined with gentle grasping, confirming no further obstructions. The transition point between dilated and collapsed bowel is identified, and the J-pouch is returned to its neutral position in the pelvis.

The surgeon performs a rigid sigmoidoscopy to confirm the detorsion of the J-pouch, noting unremarkable mucosa and slight stricturing at the anastomosis, which is gently dilated. The pouch is then desufflated, and a pexy is performed to attach the J-pouch to the pelvic sidewall using seromuscular and superficial bites, ensuring minimal tension. Additional sutures are placed for reinforcement, avoiding vascular structures, and the orientation and laxity of the J-pouch are assessed by lifting the uterus.
The surgeon places two pelvic drains and removes the trocars, marking the completion of the laparoscopic procedure. The surgical field is cleared, and the patient is prepared for recovery.

In conclusion, J-pouch torsion is an event that can be completely addressed laparoscopically with ease, and one should always be suspicious of torsion as a diagnosis if they present repeatedly for an anastomotic stricture dilation, but with continued outflow obstruction soon after.
